videos.title: GENIUS Act Explained: What the New Stablecoin Law Means for Crypto
videos.text: What if I told you big companies like Walmart and Citi Group might soon have their own cryptocurrencies, and this is 100% legal? That’s right. On July 18th, 2025, a new law called the GENIUS Act was signed. It gives private companies the green light to create stablecoins, a kind of digital dollar. Will it mean safer, easier digital money, or will it create chaos like we haven’t seen in over 100 years? Let’s find out. We all know how regular crypto like Bitcoin can swing wildly in price. One week it’s up $5,000, and the next week it’s down $10,000. Stablecoins are different. They’re supposed to stay the same value as the U.S. dollar. One stablecoin = one dollar. Simple, right? But until now, stablecoins were kind of like the Wild West: no real rules, no protections. That’s where the GENIUS Act comes in. It creates the first official rules for stablecoins in the US. Now, if a company wants to issue stablecoins, they have to keep real money in the bank to back it, get audited to prove they’re not faking it, and report how much cash they actually have every month. It’s not perfect, but it’s way safer than before. But here's where it gets interesting. Big banks like JPMorgan and Citigroup are already planning to launch their own stablecoins. Even Amazon and Walmart are exploring the idea. That’s huge. Why? Because these aren’t random crypto startups. They’re the same companies you use for your credit cards, loans, and even groceries. Now they’re stepping into the crypto world, legally. You might soon be able to buy digital dollars right from your regular bank, or even while shopping on Amazon. And since these companies already have the tech, the lawyers, and the trust, they could take over fast. Imagine buying coffee using Amazon Coins. Or paying rent with JPMorgan Digital Dollars. But not everyone’s excited. Some experts say this could bring back a disaster from the 1800s called the Free Banking Era, when any bank could print its own money. It was a total mess. Some dollars were worthless and there was no way for people to find out which money was real. Now picture this: A company says it has real money backing its stablecoin… but it doesn’t. People panic. They all try to cash out at once. Boom, a digital bank run. It spreads fast, like social media panic during a bad storm. One coin falls, then everyone starts selling every coin. Even regular crypto like Bitcoin could crash because of the fear. And what if every store only accepts certain coins? Walmart Coin here, Amazon Coin there. It could get really confusing, really fast. If things go bad, the government might have to step in and bail out companies. And that could mean your tax dollars are on the line. So what does this mean for you? The GENIUS Act opens the door for a new kind of digital money: backed by big names and with better rules. But these are still early days. It could go really well, or really wrong. Here’s what you should do if you plan to dip your toes in stablecoins. Watch how banks and the government actually roll this out. Keep some savings in old-school, rock-solid places. And don’t go all in on any one coin or platform. Because like it or not, we’re entering a brand new financial era, and you get to choose how much of it you want to be part of. While you’re waiting to see how stablecoins pan out, you don’t have to sit on the sidelines. Use this time to up your investing game. Watch this video to learn 80% of investing in under 8 minutes.

videos.title: What the GENIUS Act means for the crypto industry
videos.text: President Trump is also taking a victory lap today, signing into law the first ever major federal cryptocurrency legislation. It was passed by the House yesterday in the Senate earlier this week with broad bipartisan support. The so-called Genius Act has been a key priority for the president, once a crypto skeptic, who now builds himself as the most pro- crypto commander-in-chief in history and whose family business is building an expanding cryptocurrency empire, even launching a memecoin earlier this year. This bill regulates just one specific kind of digital currency called stable coins, aimed at making them more accessible and more mainstream. Here's what Trump said earlier today when he was signing that bill into law. This is really a big day. This afternoon, we take a giant step to cement the American dominance of global finance and crypto technology as we sign the landmark Genius Act into law. >> We want to get right to ABC's senior White House correspondent Selena Wang and coinage host and financial reporter Zach Guzman. Welcome into you both. and Selena, why has the Genius Act been such a key legislative priority of the presidents? I mean, at one point there was this question as to whether or not it would even get a vote in the House. It had this procedural stall and President Trump, as he's done before, got on the phone and worked on holdouts. Why does he care so much about this? >> Yeah, Jay, you know the president's strategy very well when it comes to members of Congress. But look, this is something that President Trump had campaigned on. He was once a crypto skeptic. Then he vowed to make America the crypto capital of the world. And on the trail, he had also pushed Congress to take action when it comes to this. And you can see there in the room, the president was surrounded by a lot of top crypto executives. This is a huge win for the cryptocurrency industry. What this does is essentially it creates this regulatory framework for stable coins. These are a type of cryptocurrency that's more stable, hence the name that's tied to a more stable asset like the US dollar. So this the goal here is to make this more mainstream and to make this more accessible. But of course, this hasn't come without controversy. President Trump and his family, they are all in on this industry. They've got several business ventures that includes a memecoin, a Trump memecoin, a Bitcoin mining firm, as well as a crypto reserve. And there's a company that the Trump family has a 60% stake in World Liberty Financial. And earlier this year, they issued their own stable coin. So, some Democrats have been arguing that President Trump's family would directly benefit from this bill. Now, the White House has insisted that there are no conflicts of interest here, that his assets are managed by a trust, one that's managed by the president's children. J. >> So, Zach, in Washington, DC here, there have been crypto ads pretty much block by block from different firms. I work on Capitol Hill every day, as Selena said. I'm only guest hosting in this chair, and there have been an army of crypto lobbyists on Capitol Hill in the last few weeks. If you if you didn't know any better, you would think this bill was about all crypto everywhere under the sun, but it's not. It's just about stable coins. So why does this bill have such a a fervor across the industry? What does it mean for the industry? >> Well, it's not just the stable coin bill that we saw get passed. It was that one and also the clarity act made it through the house. I think you know the reason why all of crypto kind of came together on this one was very much to see progress. President Trump has promised that this would be uh you know the birth of the crypto capital of the world but didn't have much to show for it. So this one finally getting across both chambers and and you know having Trump uh signing this one I think is is fairly important to show that the crypto industry can get down to DC and sell people on this no longer being an industry or a business that is negative for Americans or you know for for business here in the US >> is the goal in the crypto industry to encourage more regulation like this in order to try to make crypto more mainstream dream. >> I think you know even just as recently as right before President Trump came into office, there was a real fear among builders in the US that this industry was going to be killed if SEC chair Gary Gensler and Democrats in the White House would be able to have their way. I think now we've reached the point where this industry just wants to be able to experiment, wants to be able to show people this technology can make things more efficient and potentially also be used to help maximize the dollar in America's dominance around the world. Uh right now we're seeing people in Latin America start to use stable coins to get access to dollars. We're seeing stable coin issuers buy US debt and hold that. Tether, the largest, is now holding more US debt than the country of Germany. So, this is a very quickly growing industry and potentially could unlock new ways for President Trump and uh really Congress to start to pull levers and help the standing of America grow around the world. >> Selena, we heard Zach just mention there the Clarity Act. That was another piece of crypto legislation that the House passed that hadn't already been passed by the Senate, so it's got to go back to that chamber or it's got to go to that chamber to be voted on. But what's in that bill? >> Yeah. So, this is more expansive. This is about a regulatory framework for cryptocurrencies more overall. And it really shifts power away from the Securities and Exchange Commission and puts more control with the Commodity Future Trading Commission. Now, you heard Zach reference this as well, but the whole industry is sort of celebrating this pivot away from the Biden administration's SEC that took a very hardline approach to the cryptocurrency industry. President Trump at that event earlier today, he was praising Paul Atkins, a new SEC chair who has taken a much friendlier approach to the industry. And there is another bill as well related to this and this one would ban the Federal Reserve from creating its own digital currency. Now, we have seen other countries around the world, their central bank start to experiment and explore creating their own digital currencies. Some crypto advocates and Republican members of Congress, they're deeply concerned about the privacy implications of that. Some of those Republican members concerned enough to at one point hold up passage of the bill to get concessions out of the White House over a central bank digital currency. Selena Wang, Zack Guzman, thank you both very much for

videos.title: The GENIUS Act in Practice: Key Questions for Stablecoin Regulation
videos.text: Hello everyone and welcome to this Fireless Society virtual event. My name is Sarah Clark and I'm assistant director of practice groups at the Federalist Society. Today we're excited to host this fest webinar called the genius act in practice key questions for stable coin regulation. We're very pleased to welcome an excellent group of panelists. The Honorable Michael Bar has been a member of the Federal Reserve Board of Governors since July 19, 2022 with a term ending January 31st, 2032. He served as vice chair for supervision of the board of governors from 2022 to 2025. Prior to his appointment to the board, he was the Joan and Samford Will Dean of the Gerald R. Ford School of Public Policy, Frank Murphy, Collegiate Professor of Public Policy and the Professor of Law at the University of Michigan Law School. He is also a founder and polka dot director of the center of finance law and policy. From 2009 to 2010, he was the US Treasury Inc's assistant secretary for financial institutions. The Honorable Summer Messenger is the CEO of Blockchain Association where she leads advocacy efforts to advance digital asset innovation and policy in Washington. She previously served as commissioner of the commodity futures trading commission nominated by pre President Biden and unanimously confirmed by the Senate. With over 20 years of experience in public policy and government affairs, she has held senior roles at the CFTC on Capitol Hill as chief of staff to Senator John Dune and is in the private sector. Alex Pollock is a senior fellow with the MEI's Institute. He previously served as principal deputy director of the office of financial research in the US Treasury Department from 2019 to 2021. He was a distinguished senior fellow with the artery institute from 2015 to 2019 and 2021. He is also a resident fellow at the American Enterprise Institute from 2004 to 2015. He was president and CEO of the Federal Home Loan Bank of Chicago from 1991 to 2004. There he invented the mortgage partnership finance program was successfully created for own mortgage credit risk sharing bargaining in 1997. His decades of banking experience include being a visiting scholar at the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis. Gregs Dzales is a partner and general counsel of M multicoin Capital. He manages all legal and policy functions for the firm and works with their portfolio companies on legal and policy strategy. Prior to joining Multicoin, he was a partner in the investment management and fintech practices at Chapman and Cutler LLP with 12 years of experience counseling clients on digital asset matters. He is a director of the blockchain association, the D5 education foundation, kindly MD, and the forom law alumni association. Our moderator, Gary Kbog, is a partner at Cahill Gordon and Randall LLP. He is also a special professor of law at the Maurice A. Dean School of Law at Hopster University where he teaches derivatives law and banking law. He is a past chair of the New York City Bar Association's Committee on Regulation of Futures and Derivatives and has over 15 years of experience as a professor teaching derivatives and banking law. If you'd like to learn more about today's moderator of speakers, their full bios can be viewed on our website.org. Throughout the program, we'll turn to the audience for questions. If you have a question, please enter into the Q&A function at the bottom of your Zoom window and we'll do our best to answer as many as we can. Finally, I'll note that as always, I'll express opinion today are those of our guest speakers at the Fireless Society. With that, Mr. Kbog, thank you so much for joining us today and I'll hand things over to you. >> Hello everybody. So glad we have everybody together. Now, don't fear that we don't see Governor Bar yet. There's a little technical glitch. We'll figure that out, but he'll sure be on. But I did want to make an invitation to each of our speakers to give a few highlevel thoughts on stable coins generally. Um, for example, you know, maybe Greg, I'll ask you to start. Maybe if you could give us a high level on stable coins and their role in the financial ecosystem, how they differ from other crypto assets, how we got to this statute that passed last year called the Genius Act. So let let me hand it over to you Greg. Maybe you can provide some orientation for us all. >> Thank you Gary and thank you to everyone in the society. Uh really really happy to be here and happy to try and set the table. Uh I will not promise to do so in under five minutes. Uh the origin story for stable coins begins not in America but in Hong Kong. In 2014, a group of developers associated with the Bitfinex crypto exchange set out to tackle an issue for Asian crypto markets. Exchanges needed to move dollar value amongst themselves quickly, settle trades in real time, and hold dollar positions. But access to banking was unreliable, expensive, and typically simply unavailable. The answer was USDT or Tether, the first stable coin, which was created and live on Bitfinex later that year. Its purpose was not retail payments but exchange deposit withdrawal and settlement infrastructure. That original use case turned out to be a profound insight. It describes not just the problems facing crypto exchanges in 2015 but also remittance senders in Nigeria, treasury managers at multinationals and eventually the financial plumbing of the world itself. And it evolved into a geopolitical imperative for America. The regulatory sto story that followed Tether's inception moved from improvisation to architecture. Tether's early years were not a regulatory success story. Anything but reserve opacity, a controversial relationship with Bitfinex and a 2021 CFTC enforcement action were failures both of accountability and regulatory uncertainty. The contrast with what came next, though, is instructive. In 2018, New York's Department of Financial Services approved the first regulated stable coins from Paxos and Gemini under the limited purpose trust company framework and circles USDC under the bit license framework. Over the following years, DFS DFS built the template 1:1 reserves and highquality liquid assets, clear redemption rights, and independent attestations that set the foundation for the Genius Act. Now, what stable coins stable coins are technically and conceptually matters enormously for the conversation we're going to have here today. Stable coins are not cryptocurrencies in the sense that Bitcoin or Ethereum or Salana are. They do not ask users to accept price volatility. They do not rely on decentralization for their integrity. In fact, they invert that design choice deliberately. A stable coin issuer holds assets, issues liabilities, manages minting and redemptions, and answers to regulators and the law. That is a recognizable financial institution. What is new is the settlement layer on which the instrument circulates. That ledger is programmable, globally, globally accessible, always on asset infrastructure that is decentralized. Distinguishing from L1 cryptocurrencies, stable coins substitute for algorithmic trust, an administered trust, a regulated issuer with verified reserves. The result is an instrument that combines the velocity, programmability, and borderlessness of crypto with the par value stability and with genius institutional credibility of a regulated dollar instrument. That is a genuinely new thing. Functionally, parties with contractual rights with a stable coin issuer may create or mint a stable coin by delivery of a dollar for one stable coin or redeem vice versa. The issuer manages the minting and redemption and holds reserves in the most highly liquid assets, typically short-term treasuries, repos, and some bank deposits. In the open market in commerce, stable coins technically have floating prices, but those are generally arbitrageed as close to $1 as possible. Issuers have a responsibility for monitoring their issuance and the parties with whom they tr transact and are capable of responding to requests to freeze or seize stable coins in the market. These mechanisms are familiar to traditional finance, but now can function on these new permissionless blockchain rails. Now, the payment implications of stable coins are profound, and we're already seeing them realized across the stack. Consider where the friction in payments actually lives. In 2015, the Federal Reserve convened its faster payments task force and launched Fed Now 8 years later in July of 23. The problems the task force was trying to solve were obvious. Americans couldn't move money instantly on weekends. Small businesses waited days for settlement. and that AC batch processing had become a structural constraint on economic velocity. The institutional solution required nearly a decade. Stable coins are solving parallel problems faster and doing so across the entire payment stack, not just the consumerf facing front end, but also the institutional plumbing. In December, for example, Visa launched USDC settlement in the US, allowing its issuer and acquirer partners to settle obligations with USDC on Salana. That is back office treasury infrastructure at the core of the global card payment infrastructure and it's already live. The front end of the consumer experience for card networks doesn't change. It's the back-end settlement layer, the place where the banks, acquirers, and networks hold preunded Nostrovostro accounts to cover the timing gaps in legacy settlement. Those are being fundamentally re-engineered. The same dynamic is playing out and will play out in collateral management. Stable coins enable atomic programmable collateral posting, eliminating the overnight gaps, margin call delays, and operational friction that characterizes today's collateral transfer infrastructure. That matters for derivatives markets, SEC lending, and repo markets. The deep institutional plumbing that most people never see, but that determines the cost of capital across the financial system. Which brings us to Genius and to where we are now. The Genius Act did three things that eluded the market for a decade. It established who can issue, at what reserve standards, and under which regulators. And it's solved with clarity the securities laws issues faced by regulated payment stable coin issuers. The legal gray zone that had suppressed institutional capital, chilled bank participation, and driven innovation offshore is gone. The OCC NPRM issued in February 25th is now turning that statute into the operational architecture for what comes next. So, while checking my watch and going a little bit over, I look forward to that the conversation today and the questions Gary has prepared for us. >> Well, well, Greg, I just want to emphasize a distinction that you raised and that's a distinction between payment rails and other types of crypto or digital assets which are are not for the purpose of payment rails. And just and I just want to emphasize for everybody listening how important that distinction is because I think a lot of the people listening think digital assets or crypto. they think Bitcoin or ETH or something like that and those are functionally different than stable coin the same underlying cryptographic technology right but functionally quite different and I and I note that you made that distinction you also mentioned Fed now and I'll be interested to you know a question that we were asked or you know why do you need stable coin if you have Fed now and and you know is there going to be any plan for example for the Fed to mandate because the Fed has the rulemaking authority to have interoperability to mandate that stable coins become comp compatible with Fed now. But let me turn it over. We're I'm so glad to see uh Governor Bar um you got a very gracious introduction. So So you you but you did you were fully introduced. So I'm just going to ask you Governor Bar to to give us some of the highlevel policy view on stable coin. >> Thanks very much Gary. It's a pleasure to be with all of you in this virtual panel and I'm sorry for joining you late. I'm glad our technical difficulties were able to be overcome. Let me just um speak at a a very high level um following up on on what Greg said. So, as Greg said, last year Congress passed the Genius Act which provides some needed clarity to issuers of stable coins about how they can fit into the regulatory framework. While there's a lot of work to do by the relevant agencies to fill in the specifics during the rulemaking process, increased regulatory certainty could lead to more rapid development of stable coins. Today, stable coins are mostly used to facilitate crypto trading and secondarily as a dollar denominated store of value in some foreign jurisdictions. Stable coins might also be used to reduce the cost of remittances between some countries, to improve the speed of managing the paperwork and processes inherent in global trade and trade finance, or to help global firms manage their treasury functions. And of course, other innovations in payments may also develop by using stable coins, tokenized deposits, the systems Gary described, real-time growth settlement systems, or other other measures. A key area of concern for stable coins, however, is the potential for stable coin use in money laundering or terrorist financing since bad actors can purchase stable coins in secondary markets that may not have customer identification requirements. And in my view, both regulatory and technological solutions will be needed to limit these risks. A second key area of concern is financial stability. While people purchasing something called a stable coin might reasonably assume that they can rely on redemption at par on demand, the quality and liquidity of the reserve assets backing into stable coins could make them vulnerable. Stable coins will only be stable if they can be reliably and promptly redeemed at par in a wide range of conditions, including during stress in the market that can put pressure on the value of otherwise liquid government debt and during periods of strain on the individual issuer or its related entities. Backing up a step, caution is warranted because we have a long and sometimes painful history of private money created with insufficient safeguards. For example, in the early 1800s during the so-called free banking area, the United States had competing forms of private money in the form of banknotes, which often traded below par. There were frequent bank runs and even financial panics. And while improvements were made in the 1860s with the National Bank Act, financial crisis persisted. The particularly severe panic of 1907, which featured a run on trust companies that offered deposit products bass by backed by less liquid assets, led eventually to the creation of the Federal Reserve system in 1913. These same run dynamics incurred in modern times with respect to money market funds which came under pressure during the global financial crisis and again with the onset of the CO 19 pandemic and as everyone on this call knows stable coins themselves have been subject to valuation pressures in recent years. So the quality and liquidity of stable coin reserves are critical to their long run viability. At the same time, stable coin issuers have an incentive to maximize the return on their reserve assets by extending the risk spectrum out as far as possible. And that incentive can increase profits in good time, but risk undermining competence during market stress. And as Greg said, to address these kinds of vulnerabilities, Congress passed the Bipartisan Genius Act. And the act's primary tool to mitigate the risk of runs is limiting permissible reserve assets to an itemized list of highquality, highly liquid assets. Tight control over reserve assets coupled with supervision, capital and liquidity requirements and other measures could enhance the stability of stable coins and make them more viable payment instruments. But success in accomplishing these goals will depend on the details of regulatory implementation. Key issues include regulation of reserve assets, the potential for regulatory arbitrage, the scope of permissible activities for stable coin issuers beyond stable coin issuance, appropriate capital and liquidity requirements, anti-moneyaundering controls, and consumer protection requirements. While the Genius Act made important progress in creating a regulatory framework for stable coins, a great deal will depend on how federal and state regulators actually implement the statute. And that's why I'm happy to be here with all of you and I very much look forward to the conversation. So, Governor Bar, I I I take it that you are suggesting great care in avoiding the historical mistakes that were made. For example, during the era of free banking where you had these wildcat banks that would issue these notes and then would, you know, people would rely on the mo notes. There was total nonfgeibility among the nodes and it created a lot of commercial disruption. Um and you see that type of mistake occurring all the way potentially through money markets where where even with money markets their use as kind of quasi money um you know leads to potential disruption and and your concern is to avoid that. >> That's right Gary and you can kind of think of stable coins as a money market fund stapled to the payment system in the form of a stable coin. So the issues are are very similar issues but the velocity has changed a great deal. >> Great. Well well thank you so much for those introductory remarks and I now turn it over to Summer Mercinger. Summer, can you give us a sense in your introductory remarks about where are we now? I know there's some significant debates going on um in in the stable coin uh universe. So if you could maybe bring us up to date and and give us your thoughts. Yeah, happy to do that and thank you for um thank you all for being here. Um always an honor to uh speak to Federal Society and these events. So where are we now? It's we are we've moved from theory to execution. You know, stable coins are here. They are real payment infrastructure. And part of that is in thanks to Congress passing the Genius Act and the president signing it into law. It brought a lot of um validity to these markets but also gave the markets the kind of additional um additional kind of push to get over that next step um to to move forward and and to um hopefully become the payment rails in the future. So, I think when you're looking at kind of where are we and what are we dealing with, you know, I look at it as we're in phase two of of genius, uh phase one was obviously getting that off off of the Senate floor, getting it to the president's desk and getting it signed. And now we're at this we're at the point where we've got to implement what the bill that Congress wrote. And there are a lot of regulators involved. Um, a lot of different rulemakings are going to be going on at the same time. and it's going to be very technical as some as we've already seen in some of these proposals. So, right now what we're working on and what we're seeing is um these proposals coming out um we've got OC, FDIC, NCUA um Treasury had a an a notice of advanced proposed rule making that they've got they will have a rule making coming out soon, a proposal out soon. And so it's looking at all those proposals across the board and figuring out what that means for stable coin issuers and those who are um those who are part of the supply the chain here to get these you know more adoption of stable coins as a payment system. Um I think one issue that you will probably hear over and over again in this conversation today is the question around yield. that's already come up and I believe Greg's remarks remarks about what is permissible. Um, Genius Act said issuers cannot pay yield but that left open a door of what about third parties? What about someone who is not this stable coin issuer? What are they allowed to do? Are they allowed to pay rewards or or have a program that incentivizes adoption? So, that's a debate that's ongoing. There's a few others in I would point mostly to the OC rulemaking that we're going to get into. So, I don't want to get ahead of our discussion. Um, but for me and and the member members I represent, we really are focused on the yield issue, getting that right. Um, we're we oddly we're dealing with it through legislation. Normally you would think uh this a problem like this would be dealt with through the regulation since we are in the implementation period but we found ourselves in a situation where we now Congress is going to kind of reopen the act and legislate on who can um provide you know we're talking rewards um incentive programs you know how can that exist and not look like a deposit account or like you're going to, you know, use stable coins as an alternative to a bank deposit. So, that debate's going on now on on Capitol Hill. We have seen language um go back and forth. There's been a recent proposal that a few of us got a chance to look at from um Senators Telus and also Brooks. I think there's some opportunity to maybe make some changes around the edges. Um, so because right now it's not it's not at a place where I think industry is ready to support. So we're hopeful that we can get it there and I think that's kind of a big step forward. And then it's just we're just focused on the rule making. I mean this is like the critical point and as we talk about the rule making that one thing I want to remind everyone is rules proposals are just that they're proposals. So if you read a proposal and you think that it is, you know, kind of siding with one side or maybe not balanced, um they're they're coming at it from they really want this information. They really need expertise. They need u robust comments. And so I think that's an important kind of point that I hope comes out of this conversation is you know look at the proposals see where you can contribute where you can offer your expertise because I think the regulators really would like to learn more and do this the right way so that we have a robust stable coin uh payment system in the in the United States. So with that I'll turn it back to Gary. >> Yes. Thank Thank you so much, Summer. And one thing I'll just observe just for everybody uh who's listening, what we're talking about stable coin rewards is, for example, I have I have a Coinbase account, a legacy account. Um and I have USDC, which is a stable coin issued by Circle and it sits in the account and I get effectively interest for that balance sitting there. Now technically it's not paid directly by circle and so so there you know the view is that it's not interest in the sense that I get interest when I leave something in a bank in a in a deposit account. Um but that's what's being referred to with rewards and those rewards could take different forms. Um and part of the discussion is well maybe we should only allow rewards for doing something with a stable coin or maybe we'll prohibit rewards if it's just sitting there um statically. But in any event, I just wanted everybody to understand that's kind of the discussion. And when Summer compared it to deposits, you can see how there are some similarities because now um it wasn't always the case, but now demand deposits are allowed to pay interest in the United States. Um so let me um think and we'll definitely get back to these topics by the way, Summer, I do have some follow-up questions for you for sure, but let me turn it over to Alex Pollock uh just maybe for some overall thoughts and everything we've heard so far. Thanks, Gary. Uh, on the overall perspective, uh, let me suggest that stable coins of course aren't really stable because even if they work perfectly, they're stable relative to the dollar and the dollar itself is not stable. uh since it continually depreciates and automatically as let's say the uh central bank depreciates the dollar it depreciates stable coins just as a kind of back row issue. So the right way to think about stable coins is part of the world fiat dollar system. uh and indeed as thinking of that uh one of the most important competitive aspects at least in other countries uh is stable coins as a competitor to the Federal Reserve since dollar stable coins are a nice substitute for $100 bills uh for making uh uh transactions around the world. that just by way of a few uh macro comments. Um in the uh in in his comments, Governor Bar mentioned the financial stability issues. I fully agree there is the risk that the stable coin will not be stable relative even to the dollar and and that was not in my opinion settled by the uh by the genius act. Um consider for example as Governor Bar said that in the 19th century a lot of states required banks to hold the their own state bonds as backing uh for their currency but the states themselves defaulted often enough on those state bonds uh and the banks also got in trouble or failed. Um likewise, it seems to me that a big issue in the Genius Act is the entangling of stable coins and banking risks, putting them together. Uh and it does this, of course, through allowing the uh reserves for stable coins to be uninsured deposits in domestic and foreign banks. uh and unsecured deposits in domestic and foreign banks are an entirely different class of risk uh than are the short-term treasury bills which dominated the at least the uh the press discussion of stable coins. Uh and such deposits introduce much more individual firm and systemic risk than do uh treasury bills. Uh we're very familiar with banking risk. Uh more than 3,000 insured depositories have failed, for example, over the last half century, and many more would have failed if it weren't for federal support and bailouts of various kinds, uh including those of 2023, which notably bailed out uh circles, stable coin, along with bailing out other uninsured depositors of Silicon Valley Bank, which as we all know was was broke. uh circles USD coin lost its peg uh and uh when it announced that it had 8% and I want to keep that 8% in in mind for a minute 8% of its reserves tied up in the collapsed lender Silicon Valley Bank and when the uh uh when the federal government bailed out. All the uninsured depositors had also bailed out. the stable coin and circle was indeed the largest beneficiary of the bail of the bailout and the Silicon Valley Bank problem could clearly happen again under the Genius Act. uh as written and enacted as Nelly Leang of Brookings and formerly of the Treasury has written uh allowing stable quote allowing stable coins to hold uninsured bank deposits creates direct two-way interconnections between the risks of banks and the risks of stable coins and it's up to the regulators now to somehow deal with this risk. Let me quickly suggest there are three the components to this we ought to think about. One is interest rate risk. The Genius Act tries to keep interest rate risk out of stable coins. But if you are holding unsecured bank deposits, the interest rate risk of the bank the deposit it is in becomes a risk of the stable coin. So there is interest rate risk to be thought about. The second is affiliation risk. Uh a bank which owns a stable coin issuer will clearly want to take the proceeds of the stable coin and deposit it in its own bank. Or conversely, a stable coin issuer would logically try to set up its own bank uh which could be its parent company and and hold deposits in that uh in that bank. In either case, the assets behind the stable coin no longer are only treasury bills. They're whatever assets are in the bank. Commercial real estate loans, uh, sovereign sovereign debt, financing private capital and and leverage buyouts or or or what have you. And a third separate issue, excuse me, is a question of dealing with foreign banks uh and the very uh different kinds of risk posed by financial systems of obviously very different uh stability around the world. Now the regulatory effort is underway. We just mentioned one one minute ago the controller of the currencies 100page or so proposed rule very dense I thought well argued in many ways but it is ironic that our our stable coins are now into 100page regulatory rules. Let me just quickly mention uh two of them. Uh the the rule recommends that a safe harbor uh would be if a stable coin issuer maintains no more I'm quoting here no more than 40% of its reserve assets with any one financial institution there's in the act there's no limit about how much of the reserves could be held in the form of deposits well 40% compare that to 8% 8% of circles reserves got in in deep trouble what about 40% %. Well, if you took it, say a 25% haircut on 40% of your reserves, you'd be worth 90 cents on the dollar. And that would be a very serious issue indeed. And the OC goes on to say that it doesn't quote, again, I'm quoting again, does not want to discourage stable coin issuers from investing reserve assets, particularly in deposits of community banks. So if you imagine stable coin deposit spread very wild widely around the financial system particularly in community banks, you get an even tighter nexus between banking risk or the risk of the banking system uh in general and and the stable uh the stable coin risk and in this entanglement of the banking risk and the stable coin risk which is I think Nelly is right it is a two-way way entanglement is certainly a very important issue for all of these forthcoming uh regulations u to address and as governor bar suggested there this all uh represents in my opinion to a serious financial stability risk for the regulations to uh contemplate and address. Thanks. >> Great. Well, thank you, Alex. And then, you know, let me Alex mentioned 3,000 insured depository institutions failing uh historically in the United States. Um, Governor Bar deposits have FDIC insurance. They have access to the Fed discount window as well as other Fed programs meant to provide liquidity. Um, stable coins have neither. Yet, both are meant to trade at par. um if stable, you know, some people are projecting stable coin issuance growing to three trillion or more. I happen to find that plausible by 2030. I you know it there's a lot of payments that could use more efficient payment rails. So it seems like that could be plausible. But if a major you know Alex was giving us a little bit of scary scenarios so so let me jump into a scary scenario. A major issuer has faces a run. What happens? We're expecting you to tell us that don't worry, the Fed will step in as lender of last resort using its 13A. I guess that's not the case. So, Governor Bar, let us know how you think that would play out. >> Well, I I think that would be a terrible idea. Um, Gary, I don't think that the Federal Reserve should be backing stable coins. I don't think that stable coins should have access to the discount window or deposit insurance. I don't think the Fed should intervene inter intervene on an emergency basis to prop up stable coins. Stable coins were supposed to be this kind of cool new thing where it's only the private sector doing it and standing on its own two feet. And I I don't think entangling government support in this makes any sense. What we need to make sure to for stable coins to be stable and used is that there's strong regulation upfront. There's strong controls on reserve requirements. There are capital requirements. There's supervision. There's controls on money laundering and terrorist financing. We need to make sure those regulations are strong upfront so that people can rely on stable coins being stable. But I I don't think it's a good idea. I think it's a terrible idea to have the government subsidize stable coins or to step in to prop up stable coins. I would be adamantly opposed to that. >> Okay. Well, thank thank you. That makes makes sense. Um, and by the way, uh, just for all of our panelists, we're going to go into a bit of a lightning round with the questions just because we have so many from the audience and they're great questions and I want to make sure we can accommodate some of them. But Greg, I mean, multicoin invest across stable coin issuers, DeFi protocols, and infrastructure players. looking at the OCC's proposed rule that we were discussing before and for the audience that's implementing this Genius Act which provides a framework a federal framework for stable coin uh for stable coins to be issued. Which proposed provisions are most likely to reshape the competitive landscape? If I needed to know what I really should pay attention to that's going to change the the landscape of the um financial uh universe, what what should I be looking at? Craig, you're on mute. >> Was going to happen to someone today. Why not me? Uh f first, let's let's acknowledge that a Genius Act regulated stable coin is one of four stable coin types that are in the ecosystem more broadly. You will have nonregulated payment stable coins that look similar but are not regulated by Genius. One example of that would be the offshore version of Tether, which is presently the largest stable coin out there. Um, the second would be what might be referred to as a decentralized stable coin, one which is purely uh endogenous to a blockchain. Uh, and a good example of that would be Maker Dow uh with the D stable coin that never touches banks, never touches dollars. The fourth version which has yet to to to have a successful implementation is an algorithmic or an undercolateralized stable coin. Those generally speaking not great. Uh we saw a major crisis in 22 around the teruna uh attempt at an algorithmic stable coin um which could have certainly had better safeguards and and rails to avoid that disaster. But what Genius does, it is addressing the US regulated payment stable coins. And the first and foremost things that that it does is it makes these kosher or halal. You you can actually start engaging with these in some with some sense of trust in that you know there is a regulatory framework. New York Department of Financial Services did a very good job. Uh but now we're bringing it to the federal level with a state path available. the actual provisions in the OCC NPRM that are are going to be very interesting to see how the comment and rulemaking process goes. The first will be the rebuttable presumption on yield. Uh yield is not very well defined. When I opened a Sunrust bank account in 1995, my first personal checking account, I got a toaster. Is that yield? Probably not. Uh I did get burned on it, but uh you know, >> totally worth it. >> Totally worth it. uh you know what is yield, what is a permitted activity, what is something that looks like deposit interest. Uh those are going to be core things to figure out largely because the most expansive potential reason uh reading of a rebuttable presumption is that you cannot engage in marketing or distribution arrangements regardless of what the enduser token experience. We should not be in a position where US regulated stable coin issuers can't deploy capital for distribution purposes and compete with their unregulated offshore counterparts. >> All right. So, quick follow Oh, go ahead, Greg. I didn't want to interrupt you. Go ahead. >> Oh, and then just a second one which I think will be very important is this question of whether one brand can have one issuer and it goes to the question of white labeling. And in particular, there are two companies in the space. Anchorage, which is OC chartered, and Paxos, which is New York limited purpose trust charter. Uh both of them have had some success in creating a white label white label infrastructure to allow people to launch uh stable coins within their framework. Whereas what is uncertain in Genius and OCC rulemaking is whether or not that will be permitted or whether or not one charter means one set of books and if you want to launch another white label stable coin you need an entirely separate charter. >> Yeah, it's a little bit surprising because Visa white labels all over the place. I mean it's a normal thing in the fintech world. It's not like it was suddenly created this idea of white labeling. Um so super quick followup. You mentioned Tether Offshore. Are there any there are provisions in the Genius Act which theoretically allow a non-bank issuer or a non US issuer to issue a stable coin? Different pathways but they both have to go through something called the STRC. Um how you know and one of the questioners here and it ties into one of the questions we got. How would you address the concern that Genius Act's zero yield environment will lead to large capital flight to the digital yawn or temple bidd options? like how do you how do you avoid how do you have a competitive landscape or is it currently competitive? >> Yeah, I think it it currently is competitive. Um, you know, we see this when we're investing in offshore jurisdictions or looking at at investing in companies that are going to target offshore jurisdictions. If you ask all eight billion 8 billion people on the planet what they most want to hold, it's US dollars. And it's not for the interest that bears off of a US bank account, there isn't much of that. What it is is they want they prefer the US dollar to their local currency. That is one of the reasons why the genius act and stable coins are an important initiative is it is a projection of geopolitical strength through the dollar as well as through US treasuries. Now, a a slight shift off of that question is what happens when we are in a very low interest rate environment. And this is something that hasn't been discussed over the course of the last 5 years. But when people were launching stable coins in 181 1920, one of the big questions was profitability from them. Uh Tether has been famously and and Circle have been famously very profitable based on their reserve holdings because we're at stable relatively robust interest rates on on treasuries or yields on treasuries. What happens if those yields collapse a little and the profitability of stable coins? These are things that that have to be factored in and there are alternative means potentially to to generate revenue including including transactionbased fees. Although most stable coin issuers today are simply earning their profits off of reserve holdings and >> Oh, go ahead, Gary. >> Greg, you put more you put more money in the banks because they'll pay you more than the Treasury will. >> And and just to be clear, everybody, what we're talking about, there's this one:1 ratio that the Genius Act requires. So, every $1 in stable coin that's issued, there needs to be a dollar of either actual dollars in a deposit account, um, or short-term treasuries. Um, and so that's that's what we're talking about, the onetoone. Now, those do pay interest. A deposit account or short-term treasuries pay interest. So, if I'm a stable coin issuer, I can make money by providing the service of the stable coin, but then taking the US dollar and and getting a return on it that somebody paid to get the stable coin issued. Um, Gary, if I if I could though, because it goes to what Alex's comment and Governor Bar's comments earlier both go to this very important component, the Genius Act, its principal function is regulating what can be in the reserve. And one of the things, Alex, that that I worry about is in a low interest rate environment, we need the Genius Act to prevent issuers from going up the risk curve in order to increase profitability. Uh so I think that's an essential component. Governor, >> totally agree with Greg. I mean, that's key. There's huge incentives, particularly in a lower rate environment, for people to stretch risk. And so you got to be very tight on the reserve requirements, >> right? Um, well, on on the subject of how tight these reserve requirements should be, Alex, I wanted to ask you about this. You've argued before that allowing stable coin reserves to be parked at uninsured deposits at parent banks. So, what we're talking about is if I'm an OC national bank, I can't, unless I'm a national trust bank, don't worry about that charter, but if I'm in a national bank, I can't directly issue a stable coin, a payment stable coin. I have to set up a subsidiary to do it. But I can take the deposits and put it at my parent bank. I can put those reserves with my parent bank. And Alex has argued that that creates a business model almost identical to the one that fueled the SNL crisis, which is the time it wasn't the Great Depression when the greatest number of banks failed. It was actually during the SNL crisis. Um, if we count savings and loan institutions as banks, um, for for that purpose. Um, >> well, they're certainly insured depositories. >> Yeah. Yeah, of course. So yeah, if we use that definition, which I think is an appropriate definition, it it was a greatest number of failures um was was was then neither the OC's nor the FDI's proposed rules prohibit directly prohibit intragroup deposit reserve arrangements. What are your thoughts? Why why should we be worried about that in your opinion? A if you think about as I tried to briefly suggest in my comments what is the real asset behind the stable coin. If you do that uh either the bank sets up its own stable coin sub or the stable coin issuer sets up its own bank theoretically a parent company issues the stable coins creates quote reserves through depositing them in the affiliated bank. What is the asset then that's behind the stable coin? Well, it's whatever the bank does and and banks do a lot more. In fact, they hard they hold hardly any um uh treasury bills in their assets. They hold all kinds of loans that are have various kinds of risks but but are often very risky. That's why banks often fail and why they're surrounded with encyclopedic reams of regulation and and institutional support that as we have discussed. So if you if you are able to simply issue your stable coins, put them in your own bank, the actual asset behind the stable coin is anything that bank does. It might be something very conservative but it might might not be. So I do think that this affiliation uh uh uh feature is something that regulation needs to address. Also if you had let's say um uh concentration limits on how much of your deposits could be in any one bank. You ought to think about affiliated banks. So, if you own if you were a a bank holding company that owned 10 banks, uh, could you have 10% of your stable coin deposits in each of your 10 banks, which would actually be in one owned and controlled entity? I don't know if you thought about that one over at the Fed. >> Does that matter much because they're all insured depository institutions, the ins one of them, if they're affiliated, will would be >> Yeah. If you're insured, >> an institution doesn't mean that your deposit is insured. Needless to say, and we're talking about deposits which are uninsured, >> right? >> I think the affiliate rules are going to matter a lot. I mean, you know, people sometimes say, why do these entities need capital at all? One reason is that they can affiliate with other entities, including banks, and you have to make sure that the capital is covering all those risks. The second is that issuers themselves can do activities beyond what is onetoone issuance. So service activities and other activities of the issuer might present risk. And then even more basically if you look at the issuer itself even with onetoone reserves as I said in my opening statement you can have serious run risk as we saw with money market funds and as we saw um with stable coins themselves. So capital is absolutely essential for all three reasons. You can't just rely on reserve requirements to have a stable stable coin. >> Let me ask Summer. >> I agree. Let me let me get a let me get a maybe a little less of a pessimistic perspective uh on this. Let me ask Summer. Summer, you have over 130 members of the blockchain association which you are CEO of. When you are going to bed, what is your single greatest source of of regulatory uncertainty that keeps you up at night and the Genius Act? I think I know what the answer is, but let's have you answer. >> Yes, I dream and yield. Um, unfortunately, it it kind of follows me everywhere I go. I because it's so important to adoption, you know, to have this is this is a marketing tool. This is what is going to drive adoption. And if we are too restrictive on third parties paying what are essentially rewards just like your credit card rewards that you mentioned Gary um or the or the toaster from the bank. If you don't have that you're not going to see the same adoption and and we are this is a competitive environment. There are offshore issuers that are the largest market right now. And if we don't have this uh adop this way to kind of fuel adoption, you're going to look you might be looking at a system where the dollar isn't the the currency reserve, you know, and that's why this is so important that we see widespread adoption um of of stable coins. And I think on the yoke side um it's it's that ability to reward people for using these payments. This is a payment system. You want to be able to reward users of you know stable coin so that you are have you get adoption you get the stable coin being used the way it was intended. Um so I think there's there's a lot of uh gray area here and that's what you know both the rulemaking is looking at what Congress is looking at and I think the the greatest you know my fear is that we are going to go too far in the direction of everything looks like yield and you're going to actually be preventing you know preventing business decisions on rewards on other um tokens that you happen to hold along with your stable stable coin. So, there's a real uh there's a real kind of fear of going too far and preventing the stable coin adoption in the US that we need to see. >> And and I just want to note this is what created the libore market. This is what created the Euro dollar market. Of course, there's restrictions on banks paying interest. Um the old red Q and it did lead to, you know, a competitive dynamic where people just swept their who could, right? Not people like me and ordinary people, but you know, large institutions that had the wherewithal swept their money into the London market where their US dollars would not be subject to the regq cap. And that's where, by the way, everybody, we got the toasters and everything else because during high inflation times, interest rates were capped. So banks were say, well, let's do rewards. These were old school rewards, but let's do rewards, whether it's a toaster or something else. But Governor Bar, we I I wanted to, and please, all the people have asked questions. I'm going to try to address as many as I can. Forgive me, I won't be able to address all of them, but I'm going to ask Governor Bar this one that we received. The Genius Acts reserve asset list is meant to be exhaustive and safe. But El Salvador has designated Bitcoin as legal tender. So does that create a backdoor where a stablecoin issuer could argue Bitcoin denominated repo qualifies as a permissible reserve asset which means one of those things a permitted stable coin issuer could in invest the dollar they get they issue a stable coin and they could invest their dollar that they got for it in um as a permissible reserve asset. So, and if so, how do regulators close that gap? And I'll note, Governor Bar, I think you addressed this in October 2025. You raised exactly this concern in one of your speeches. Do you want to elucidate on that and and along with any suggestions on maybe what the Federal Reserve might be able to do or is just this is does a statutory text just tie everybody's hands? >> Gary, um, thank you for that. Yes, I I raised this exact issue while genius was being debated and then again more formally in a speech in October of last year because it's so critical for reserve assets to be tightly cabin to really safe assets for stable coins actually to be stable and this what I think of as a loophole. you might have an issue or argue that Bitcoin repo is a permissible reserve asset for precisely the logic and and reason that your questioner described. There's a you know the possibility that that Bitcoin could be treated as legal tender in other jurisdictions under the definition of money in the act which was changed at the last minute in the process. money was broadened in a way to include um some things that most people who are going to purchase this stable coin would not have thought could conceivably be stable. So I think it's really critical that the regulators use their authority to really cabin reserve assets and to block any argument by issuers that Bitcoin repo is a suitable asset for reserves that creates the kind of circular risk that Alex was talking about in the banking sector creates it in the crypto sector. That would be a big mistake. And you you saw in the OC's, you know, proposal in this way, they reserve judgment on this. They basically said, we get to decide what a permissible reserve asset is, and we're going to reserve judgment on this question that hasn't yet been kind of posed to us. I think it's really important not to let that kind of loophole um survive the regulatory >> Okay. Well, thank thank you. Uh I want to ask uh Greg something that you know to build into I'm going to build on this but we got asked how should the AML um regulatory framework for stable coins differ from that for traditional financial institutions or other virtual assets like cryptocurrencies if at all and you know I want to tie into that the the question the OCC's proposed rule did not address whether D5 protocol operators or front-end interface operators and folks on the audience don't know what those are, that's okay. I'll I'll explain in a moment. Qualify as so-called digital asset service providers. Um, leaving open a question of, you know, who is going to be responsible? What are the BSA AML requirements for folks who are in the DeFi world and and just to to explain to the audience? So if if I'm minting a stable coin token, which means creating one and issuing it, I I have to do KYC and AML. Um so I know who my customer is. I know we're not money laundering. But once it's out there, there becomes a question of when are when do responsibilities exist and when they don't. So Greg, do you want to take that on? >> Sure. And actually it's app timing. This morning I was at a a TFI Treasury round table addressing illicit finance and digital assets. Uh they've been having a handful of these in DC and New York over the last year or so. And it it does raise new issues generally for for digital assets, but it also raises new tools. This technology has radical transparency and immediacy of information. near realtime monitoring becomes available and that opens up new tools for stable coin issuers in particular and folks at Treasury and other uh government institutions that want to be able to monitor things and ingest data in real time. The important distinction between stable coins and a traditional digital asset is stable coins are administered digital assets and we see this on a regular basis. uh Circle, Tether, all of the big stable coin issuers will respond to lawful requests to freeze assets and do have the ability to to seize uh assets in transit uh or at least while they're at rest. And that's an important distinction when we look at secondary markets in stable coins. And that's another distinguishing feature between stable coins and traditional runs is you don't only run to the issuer for redemption. You also have the possibility of exiting on secondary markets or utilizing those stable coins for personal use or payments purposes. Um that doesn't change, Alex, your your your comments about pegs uh deping from time to time, but arbitrage works reasonably well. The circle s uh silvergate uh was an outlier for a variety of reasons we don't have time to go into. But uh when we look at who the OC has sought to regulate in their NPRM is these third-party digital asset service providers. And when we look at DeFi protocols, a crypto trading platform, a Coinbase or or what or Kraken, what have you, those are clearly DASPs, custodians, clearly DASPs. When we get into DeFi, it's a little bit different. You have the protocol itself which is if it's truly decentralized is not operated by any party and then you have frontends who will in some form have certain responsibilities but the AML component of that is still to be written and will follow from from market structure. For stable coin issuers their AML requirements are going to be particularly with the people they transact with on minting and redeeming and then responding to government requests for freezes. Jerry, I'll just add quickly. The more the tighter those controls become on stable coins, which it would be good, I think, the more competitive hope there is for $100 bills to maintain their historical role. >> Right. >> Well, I just want to say that a great conversation always leaves you yearning for more. And that is quite um evidently the case here. So many policy questions. They really are almost endless. This has been extremely interesting discussion. I truly wish we had another hour, but I'm afraid we don't. All of those people who asked these excellent, excellent questions, I'm sorry we couldn't address them all. But thank you everyone for attending and thank you most of all to this great panel. Thank you very much everybody for taking your time for doing this. And let me hand it over now to Sarah Clark to round us out. >> Thanks Gary. On behalf of the Fireless Society, thank you so much to Governor Bar, Miss M. Mercinger, Mr. Pollock, and Mr. Zalis for speaking with us today and to Mr. Colbog for moderating. We're so grateful for your time and expertise on this important topic. Thank you to our audience for joining us. We really appreciate your participation. You can also stay up to date with other announcements and upcoming webinars on our website, pet fes.org. Thank you once more for tuning in and we are returned.

videos.title: The Genius Act Explained  NEW Stablecoin Cryptocurrency Law Bill
videos.text: Hey, how's it going everyone? Welcome to Finance with A. In this video, I'm going to give you an overview, summary, explanation of the Genius Act and what this act could mean for you if you're into cryptocurrency and stable coins specifically. Be sure to like the video down below and subscribe to the channel if you're not already. Very much appreciate it if you do either one of those. Let's get into more specific information. Now, what is the Genius Act? And this act is a long act as well. Several pages right here. Check it out right there on the screen. screen. I'm scrolling through it, scrolling through it, scrolling through it. Yeah. So, let's get an overview of what this act could mean for you, especially if you're into stable coins with cryptocurrency. Now, this act was signed into law by President Trump on July 18th, 2025, and it is the first federal law to regulate stable coins. Now, if you're wondering what are stable coins, stable coins are a type of digital asset that's pegged to the dollar. Basically, one per one. So, typically one stable coin is going to equal $1. There's various different types of stable coins out there. One of the main ones is USDC, which was issued by Circle, which was in partner with Coinbase and everything. I actually have a detailed video of pros and cons about USDC on Coinbase. You can check that video out. Link in the description. Of course, some of that's going to kind of change as far as some of the cons because with this act, if Circle, the issuer for USDC, and you know, with Coinbase, if they follow through with some of the things on the act, some of those cons are going to turn into pros. So, things are going to change right there for these stable coins. in cryptocurrency space for sure with the Genius Act. Now, this Genius Act wasn't as split as some of the other bills that have passed through the White House and been signed by the president. This was kind of bipartisan. There are Democrats and Republicans that voted for this bill in the House and Senate. So, it wasn't split just like, hey, this is a Republican thing. It's a Democrat thing, anything like that. This video, of course, unbiased, not getting to either side of that, but just wanted to mention that. So, purpose of this law, this is to make the United States the global leader in digital assets and cryptocurrency innovation. Stable coins are one of the main types of cryptocurrencies. With this Genius Act, it kind of propels the United States to the forefront of cryptocurrency with the innovation of that of pretty much like putting a stamp on it saying, "Hey, we're making this even more official." And they're also hoping that this strengthens the US dollar status as the world's reserve currency. Because if you stamp this down and say, "Hey, you know, stable coins are going to be regulated pegged to the US dollar." Well, stable coins are used all over the world. This isn't just a United States thing. USDC, Coinbase, you know, um, Kraken, Binance, all these different, you know, cryptocurrency platforms. They're not just for the United States. They're all over the world. There's more than that. There's a lot in cryptocurrency and but there all over stable coins all over. We have the United States coming in and putting a federal law on stable coins or they're having it where the stable coins will be backed by US dollars or, you know, things that are related to US dollars like short-term treasuries and things like that. We'll get into that. But so this is kind of propelling US dollar sum up even more to be being like the world's currency and also this genius act is going to protect consumers in this growing digital finance space because there's definitely some things that have happened in cryptocurrency some sketchy things and where consumers have been hurt. You know me personally I actually had money in Voyager and BlockFi they went bankrupt and so it was a whole hassle ordeal of you were part of that you know what I mean. So, it was a while before we got some money back from that. And it wasn't even like the actual amount that it should have been because, you know, cryptocurrency was moving while that was going on. You know, it was going up, it was going down, and we couldn't sell when we wanted to because the places have these platforms have gone bankrupt. So, it just had to go through the whole legal process of that and the creditors and all that. And then eventually start to get paid out some. But I won't get into all that. That was a whole ordeal. But this Genius Act will hopefully help some of that regulation going. And there's actually two more acts going on through Congress in terms of cryptocurrency to put more of like federal, you know, regulations on it. So, but this video right here just about the Genius Act and also they're hoping this Genius Act combats illicit finance using stable coins. You know, crack down, you know, on people using stable coins for fraud, scams, different things like that. So, that is the overview right there of what the Genius Act is and why the United States pretty much put this out in the forefront and approved this act and why the president signed this act to go into effect. So yeah, pretty major right here. First federal law to regulate stable coins. Now, let's get into who can issue stable coins and the requirements based on this new Genius Act. So they've kind of separated in two different types of institutions that will be able to issue stable coins. And of course, this act could have amendments. Things could definitely change with this act, but as of right now, this is what's going on. Who can issue stable coins through the Genius Act to be like, you know, Genius Act approved and compliant. So first type here we got the insured depository institutions. Now of course these are traditional FDIC insured banks and credit unions that have that you know NCUA insurance. So traditional type of banks that are out there that already existing or ones that are going to become FDIC insured type of banks and insured credit unions. That's the first type right here. Now they have rules and requirements as well. They just can't say hey we're we're already a bank. We're already FDIC insured here. We're going to issue a stable coin. Now they have to follow strict federal rules including capital liquidity and risk management standards which a lot of banks you know are already following that you know the banks have their stress test and different various things they have to make sure they do especially since the you know 2008 recession the crisis the housing crisis all that where there's more laws and implementations on banks to follow certain requirements now this of course is separate than that but I'm just telling you of course how banks have to follow certain rules of course they just can't open up and say hey we're a bank just come make an account with us so you know they have to follow strength rules. They're going to have to with the Genius Act as well. Now, under this Genius Act, they can issue stable coins under their existing bank license. Do the banks want to do that? Are they going to be able to back up the stable coin? Various factors they got to think about, of course, but do they even want to get into cryptocurrency? You know, various factors banks will have to think about in credit unions in terms of do they want to, you know, follow through and be compliant with the Genesis Act because this is not saying you have to. So, this is one thing with the Gans Act. You don't have to do this if you're not going to issue stable coins, you know, be a part of that with cryptocurrency and everything. This is if they want to or if they're already issuing stable coins. We'll get into that too a little bit later for as the timeline for this. Now, the second type of institution that can issue stable coins per the Genius Act are going to be OCC charter non-bank stable coin issuers.
